content.problemStatement:
Given a string containing uppercase English letters (possibly with duplicates), generate all unique permutations of the string (in lexicographical order).


Input Format

A single string SSS containing uppercase English letters


Output Format

Print all unique permutations of the string in a new line, space-separated.
constraints: S contains only uppercase English letters
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a string S consisting of only uppercase English letters (1 ≤ |S| ≤ 9).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output all unique permutations of the given string in lexicographical order, separated by a single space. Print a newline after the permutations of each test case.

sampleTestCases.output: ABC ACB BAC BCA CAB CBA
sampleTestCases.explanation: The string \"ABC\" has three distinct letters, so all 3! = 6 permutations appear in lexicographical order.
Because the letter 'A' appears twice, only three distinct permutations exist. They are listed in lexicographical order.

solution.approach: Sort the characters of the string. Then generate permutations in lexicographical order while skipping duplicates. Two common ways: (1) use C++/Python built‑in next_permutation/itertools.permutations on the sorted string – it automatically produces lexicographic order and handles duplicates when the input is sorted; (2) implement a classic backtracking that at each position tries every unused character, but skips a character if it is the same as the previous one and the previous copy has not been used yet. Both approaches run in O(P·N) time where P is the number of distinct permutations and N is the length of the string, and use O(N) auxiliary space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(P·N) per test case, where P is the number of unique permutations (worst case N! when all characters are distinct).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) auxiliary space for recursion/iteration buffers, plus O(P·N) for the output strings (required by the problem).

code.Python3:
import sys
def unique_permutations(s):
    chars = sorted(s)
    n = len(chars)
    used = [False]*n
    cur = ['']*n
    res = []
    def backtrack(depth):
        if depth == n:
            res.append(''.join(cur))
            return
        for i in range(n):
            if used[i]:
                continue
            if i>0 and chars[i]==chars[i-1] and not used[i-1]:
                continue
            used[i]=True
            cur[depth]=chars[i]
            backtrack(depth+1)
            used[i]=False
    backtrack(0)
    return res

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().splitlines()
    if not data:
        return
    t = int(data[0])
    out_lines = []
    for i in range(1, t+1):
        s = data[i].strip()
        perms = unique_permutations(s)
        out_lines.append(' '.join(perms))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))
if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
